raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

Sleep won't come. You try counting sheep. The sheep turn into cards. The cards turn into debts. The debts turn into a spiral.
You lie there, eyes wide open, listening to your own breathing and the distant hum of the highway.
Somewhere out there, normal people are sleeping in beds. In houses. With futures.
You wonder if they know how lucky they are. Probably not. Nobody does until it's gone.

The casino never sleeps. Neither do you, it seems.

You're in a car—your car—and water is pouring in through every crack.
You can't open the doors. The pressure is too much. The windows won't break.
The water reaches your waist. Your chest. Your neck. Your mouth.
You scream but water fills your lungs instead of air.
You wake up underwater.
THIS ISN'T A DREAM. Your car is IN THE RIVER. You must have rolled down the embankment while sleeping.
The water is at your waist. Rising fast. You fumble for the window crank.
It's stuck. You slam your elbow against the glass. Again. Again. AGAIN.
It cracks. Shatters. Water rushes in but you have an opening.
You squeeze through, cutting yourself on the glass, and kick toward the surface.
Air. Sweet, precious air. You drag yourself onto the bank and collapse.
By morning, the wagon is a drowned wreck-half-sunk, flooded through, and useless as transportation.
You can salvage your life, but not your ride. Most of what you kept inside is ruined or swept away. But you're alive.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You feel anxious tonight.

As you're walking along the side of the road, something catches your eye. A flash of white, darting between the bushes. A rabbit!
Without thinking, you give chase. The little creature bounds ahead of you, zigging and zagging through the underbrush with seemingly effortless grace.
You run and run, but no matter how fast you go, the rabbit stays just out of reach. Its white tail bobs mockingly in the moonlight.
Finally, you stop, hands on your knees, gasping for breath. When you look up, the rabbit is gone, vanished into the night like it was never there.
You trudge back to your wagon, defeated. But something tells you this isn't the last time you'll see that rabbit.

He holds up a single white pill between his thumb and forefinger.

"You look like a collector. A finder of rare things."
He glances around nervously, then leans closer.
"Name's Gus. Grimy Gus, they call me. Got a little shop down on Fifth and Nowhere. "
"If you ever find yourself with... unusual items... things that don't belong in the light of day... I can make them disappear. For a fair price."
He taps his nose conspiratorially.
"Collectibles, trinkets, treasures. Things you picked up on your... adventures. I don't ask questions. Just cash on the barrel."
He hands you a grimy business card through the crack in the window. It reads: "Grimy Gus's Pawn Emporium - We Buy What Others Won't"
"Come by sometime. You won't regret it. Probably."
He shuffles off into the morning mist before you can respond.

You stretch out and feel every vertebra in your spine crack in the most satisfying way. Today is going to be good.
You're minding your own business when a familiar figure appears from behind a tree.
Striped shirt. White face. The mime is back.
This time, his act is different. More elaborate. He's set up an invisible stage
with invisible curtains. He even does an invisible drum roll.
The performance begins. He mimes waking up in a car. Your car. He mimes playing cards.
Winning. Losing. He mimes meeting different people—
Wait, is he doing Jameson? He's pretending to ride a horse. That's definitely Jameson.
Now he's being Betsy. He's on all fours going 'moo.' Well, mouthing 'moo.' He's still a mime.
The mime finishes with a new bit: he pulls an invisible rope and lifts himself off the ground,
dangling in the air. Then he falls, catches himself, and takes a bow.
He's sweating under the face paint. This guy takes his craft seriously.

You wake up with energy. Like, real energy. Not 'I didn't die in my sleep' energy.
The sound of a tractor barrels closer. As you jump up from your seat, you see the tractor getting closer to your wagon.
The tractor drives beside your vehicle, and pushes right up against you, grinding the paint off your car. That's just mean.
You look up at the driver to see a beautiful black and white cow. Good god, it's Betsy. Why, Betsy, why.
The cow moos at you aggressively, and you roll down the window.
Betsy stares into your soul, then looks over at the seat next to you. It appears Betsy is interested in your pile of money.
Do you feed Betsy?
You park your wagon right up front next to the entrance, and step out towards the doors.
When you enter into the lobby, you're immediately hit with the strong smell of hand sanitizer in the air.
The carpets are dull and brown, the light above you is flickering,
and the walls are filled with posters telling you to 'Floss More Often!' and 'Wash Your Hands Before You Eat!'
If you didn't know any better, you would have guessed you were on a movie set.
Walking towards the front desk, you see a cheery old lady, who looks up from her computer to smile at you.
Her gray hair covers her glasses, and her hand trembles as she hands you a pen and a clipboard with some paperwork.
Of course it's paperwork.
After filling out your information, you walk back to the front desk, and hand the lady the clipboard. She smiles, and begins to speak to you.
I see you're here for a checkup. The Doctor will see you now.
Hey there champ! How are you? Doing all right? Let's check you out and make sure you're all up to snuff.
Open wide... yep, those white patches on your tonsils are unmistakable. Strep. Ten days of antibiotics for you.
Well, that seems to be everything. You still appear a little worse for wear, but this medicine should do the trick.
You walk back to the front desk to checkout.

You find Dr. Feelgood in his usual spot. But something's different.
He's not smiling today. He's counting money.
"Bad news, friend. My supplier raised prices. Supply chain issues. ""Inflation. The usual excuses."
"New price is $150. I know. I know. But I'm still cheaper than a hospital."
Your hands are shaking. Not from the cold. From the need.
You don't have enough. You tell him. Beg him.
"I'm a businessman, not a charity. Come back when you've got the cash."
He packs up his bag and walks away. The withdrawal hits within hours.

Something's definitely wrong with your body. That's for certain.

Then — snap — your body releases. You bolt upright, gasping.
The back seat is empty. Nothing there. Just the dark.
You don't sleep for the rest of the night. You sit with every light on and your eyes open,
watching the back seat in the rearview mirror.
Nothing comes. But the cold lingers.
Your back feels like it's being torn apart.

He doesn't notice you at first.

"Jesus! You scared me. What are you doing back here?"
You nod at the paper. It's a hospital bill. The number at the bottom has five digits.
Kyle looks at the bill, then at you.
"You wouldn't understand. Nobody does. I've been... taking money. From the register. "
"Small amounts. For my mom. She needs it more than Hendricks does."
Kyle looks at you with desperate eyes.
"You're not gonna tell anyone, right?"

"You wouldn't understand. Nobody does. I've been... taking money. From the register. "

"Let's embark on a journey, a meandering path not unlike the serpentine wanderings of a leaf caught in a capricious autumn breeze, which, as it dances to the silent music of nature, reminds us of the unpredictable choreography of existence. This leaf, let's call it Gerald, flutters with an elegance borne of happenstance, a fragile vessel for the whims of the wind. Gerald's journey is not linear, nor is it bound by the rigid expectations of society's ceaselessly grinding gears. Instead, Gerald twirls, dips, and soars, embracing the chaos with a grace we can only aspire to. Now, consider the ant, industrious and steadfast, a creature of purpose and communal toil. Our ant, whom we'll name Beatrice, marches diligently on her quest for sustenance, her life a testament to the virtues of hard work and persistence. Yet, in the grand tapestry of the cosmos, what is Beatrice but a speck, a mere blip on the infinite canvas of the universe? And yet, does her insignificance in the face of the vast unknown render her efforts moot? I posit that it does not, for in the grand scheme, all actions, large and small, contribute to the intricate mosaic of existence. But back to Gerald, who by now has traversed the convoluted landscapes of our imagination, touching upon the existential questions that haunt the periphery of our consciousness. What can we learn from Gerald and Beatrice? Is it their resilience, their unyielding will to persist in the face of the insurmountable odds stacked against them by the very nature of their existence? Or is it, perhaps, the simple beauty of their dance, a reminder that life, in all its complexity and confusion, offers moments of sublime beauty, fleeting and precious, to be cherished and remembered? As Gerald finally comes to rest upon the earth, joining Beatrice in the eternal cycle of life and death, we are reminded that all journeys, whether they be of leaves or ants or the human heart, are interconnected in the grand, bewildering dance of the cosmos. So, what was the point of this story, you may ask? Well, that, my friend, is entirely up to you."

He points down the road. Points at the tree line. Points at a spot across the street
where — was someone standing there? You look, but there's nobody now.
The mime grabs your arm. His grip is surprisingly strong. His eyes, under all that white paint,
are scared. Genuinely scared.
He flips to a third sign: "BE CAREFUL. THEY DON'T LIKE WHAT YOU'RE DOING."
Before you can react, the mime hears something. He snaps his head to the left,
drops the signs, and sprints away faster than any mime should be able to run.
You pick up the signs. On the back of the last one, in small handwriting:
"My name is Gerald. If they find out I told you, I'm done. -G"
Something's definitely wrong with your body. That's for certain.

The carnival is still there. You're drawn back. The lights pulse in a rhythm
that matches your heartbeat.
This time, you head for the fortune teller's tent. Purple velvet. Gold tassels.
A sign reads: "MADAME VERA SEES ALL. TIPS APPRECIATED."
Inside, a woman sits behind a crystal ball. She's ancient. Her eyes are milky white.
The tent smells like incense and regret.
"Sit. I've been expecting you."
You sit. The crystal ball hums.
"You live in your car. You play blackjack for money. You've met people who ""changed you and people who tried to break you."
She's not guessing. She's reading.
"The officer who brings coffee... he lost someone. He found you instead."
"The silent man with the painted face... he's safe now. Don't worry."
Madame Vera leans forward.
"You want to know if it gets better. That's what everyone asks."
"You live in your car. You play blackjack for money. You've met people who ""changed you and people who tried to break you."
